name: Crohn's Disease
signs:
Abdominal pain (RLQ common)
Chronic diarrhea (often non-bloody)
Weight loss
Fatigue
Perianal disease (fistulas, abscesses, skin tags)
Mouth ulcers
Skip lesions on endoscopy
Cobblestone mucosa
Extraintestinal: arthritis, erythema nodosum, uveitis
treatment:
1. Induction: corticosteroids or biologics
2. Maintenance: immunomodulators or biologics
3. Step-up vs top-down approach based on severity
4. Surgery for complications (strictures, fistulas, abscesses)
5. Nutritional support
6. Smoking cessation (worsens Crohn's)
7. Perianal disease: antibiotics + biologics + surgery
meds:
Budesonide 9mg daily (ileal/right colon)
Prednisone 40-60mg daily taper (moderate-severe)
Azathioprine 2-2.5mg/kg daily
Methotrexate 25mg SC weekly
Infliximab 5mg/kg IV at 0,2,6 weeks then q8wk
Adalimumab 160mg SC then 80mg then 40mg q2wk
Vedolizumab 300mg IV at 0,2,6 wks then q8wk
Ustekinumab 6mg/kg IV then 90mg SC q8wk
ddx: Ulcerative colitis, IBS, Celiac disease, Intestinal TB, Appendicitis, Diverticulitis, Lymphoma, Behcet disease
labs:
Colonoscopy with biopsies (skip lesions, transmural inflammation, granulomas)
CRP/ESR elevated
Fecal calprotectin (>250 suggests active inflammation)
CBC (anemia)
Albumin (malnutrition)
CT/MR enterography (small bowel involvement, strictures)
Vitamin B12, folate, iron, vitamin D levels
TBST before biologics
notes:
Can affect any part of GI tract (mouth to anus), most common in terminal ileum
Transmural inflammation → fistulas, strictures, abscesses
Skip lesions distinguish from UC (continuous involvement)
Smoking worsens Crohn's (opposite of UC)
Screen for TB and hepatitis B before starting biologics
Colonoscopy surveillance for CRC starting 8 years after diagnosis
description: A chronic inflammatory bowel disease that can affect any part of the digestive tract, causing transmural inflammation that leads to abdominal pain, diarrhea, and complications like fistulas and strictures.

name: Ulcerative Colitis
signs:
Bloody diarrhea
Rectal urgency and tenesmus
Abdominal cramping (LLQ)
Fever (severe flares)
Weight loss
Extraintestinal: primary sclerosing cholangitis, arthritis, pyoderma gangrenosum, erythema nodosum
treatment:
1. Mild-moderate: 5-ASA (mesalamine) oral + rectal
2. Moderate-severe: corticosteroids for induction, biologics/immunomodulators for maintenance
3. Refractory: cyclosporine or infliximab
4. Toxic megacolon: IV steroids, antibiotics, surgical consult
5. Colectomy is curative
6. CRC surveillance colonoscopy q1-2 years after 8 years
meds:
Mesalamine 2.4-4.8g daily (oral) + suppository/enema
Prednisone 40-60mg daily taper
Azathioprine 2-2.5mg/kg daily
Infliximab 5mg/kg IV
Adalimumab 160mg then 80mg then 40mg q2wk
Tofacitinib 10mg BID induction then 5mg BID
Vedolizumab 300mg IV
Cyclosporine 2mg/kg IV (acute severe refractory)
ddx: Crohn's disease, Infectious colitis (C. diff, CMV, E. coli), Ischemic colitis, Radiation colitis, Diverticulitis, IBS, Colorectal cancer
labs:
Colonoscopy with biopsies (continuous inflammation from rectum, pseudopolyps, loss of haustra)
Stool studies (rule out infection, C. diff)
CRP/ESR
Fecal calprotectin
CBC (anemia, leukocytosis)
Albumin
LFTs and ANCA/ASCA (distinguish from Crohn's)
p-ANCA positive in 65-70% UC
Abdominal XR (toxic megacolon screening)
notes:
Always starts at rectum, extends proximally in continuous pattern
Mucosal/submucosal inflammation (unlike transmural in Crohn's)
Toxic megacolon: colon >6cm, systemic toxicity → emergent colectomy
PSC association: 5% of UC patients, increases CRC risk further
Total proctocolectomy with ileal pouch-anal anastomosis (IPAA) is curative
CRC risk: cumulative 2% at 10yr, 8% at 20yr, 18% at 30yr
description: A chronic inflammatory bowel disease that causes inflammation and ulcers along the inner lining of the large intestine and rectum, typically presenting with bloody diarrhea.

name: Cirrhosis
signs:
Jaundice
Ascites
Spider angiomata
Palmar erythema
Gynecomastia
Caput medusae
Splenomegaly
Asterixis (hepatic encephalopathy)
Dupuytren contracture
Terry nails
Testicular atrophy
treatment:
1. Treat underlying cause (abstinence, antivirals)
2. Ascites: sodium restriction + diuretics
3. Variceal screening with EGD
4. HCC screening: US + AFP q6 months
5. Hepatic encephalopathy: lactulose + rifaximin
6. SBP prophylaxis if prior episode
7. Liver transplant evaluation for decompensated disease
meds:
Spironolactone 100mg daily + furosemide 40mg daily (100:40 ratio for ascites)
Lactulose 15-30mL TID (encephalopathy)
Rifaximin 550mg BID
Nadolol 20-80mg daily (variceal prophylaxis)
Norfloxacin 400mg daily (SBP prophylaxis)
Albumin 1.5g/kg day 1, 1g/kg day 3 (SBP treatment)
ddx: CHF (cardiac cirrhosis), Budd-Chiari syndrome, Hepatic fibrosis without cirrhosis, Hepatocellular carcinoma, Fatty liver disease
labs:
LFTs (AST/ALT may be normal in advanced disease)
CBC (thrombocytopenia from hypersplenism)
INR/PT prolonged
Albumin decreased
Bilirubin elevated
Fibroscan/elastography (fibrosis staging)
Hep B/C serologies, iron studies, ceruloplasmin, ANA/ASMA/AMA
MELD score: bilirubin, INR, creatinine
US with Doppler q6 months for HCC screening
notes:
Child-Pugh: A (5-6), B (7-9), C (10-15) based on bilirubin, albumin, INR, ascites, encephalopathy
MELD score: determines transplant priority
Decompensation: ascites, variceal bleed, encephalopathy, jaundice
Median survival compensated 12+ years, decompensated 2 years
SBP diagnosis: ascitic fluid PMN >250/mm3
Avoid NSAIDs, aminoglycosides, ACEi in decompensated cirrhosis
description: End-stage liver scarring where normal liver tissue is replaced by scar tissue, impairing the liver's ability to function and causing complications like fluid buildup, bleeding, and confusion.

name: Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease
signs:
Heartburn (retrosternal burning)
Regurgitation
Dysphagia
Chronic cough
Hoarseness
Dental erosion
Chest pain (non-cardiac)
Worse after meals, lying down
Relieved by antacids
treatment:
1. Lifestyle modifications: weight loss, elevate HOB, avoid triggers
2. PPI trial (first-line for moderate-severe)
3. H2 blockers for mild symptoms or on-demand use
4. EGD if alarm symptoms or refractory
5. Anti-reflux surgery (Nissen fundoplication) for refractory GERD
6. Step-down PPI therapy after 8 weeks
meds:
Omeprazole 20mg daily (or BID if severe)
Pantoprazole 40mg daily
Famotidine 20mg BID (H2 blocker)
Calcium carbonate/antacids PRN
Sucralfate 1g QID (mucosal protection)
Baclofen 10mg TID (reduces transient LES relaxations)
ddx: Peptic ulcer disease, Eosinophilic esophagitis, Esophageal cancer, Achalasia, Coronary artery disease, Functional dyspepsia, Gastroparesis
labs:
Clinical diagnosis (PPI trial is diagnostic and therapeutic)
EGD: erosive esophagitis, Barrett esophagus, stricture
Biopsies if Barrett suspected
24h pH monitoring (gold standard for quantifying reflux)
Esophageal manometry (pre-surgical)
H. pylori testing
notes:
Alarm symptoms (need EGD): dysphagia, weight loss, GI bleeding, anemia, vomiting, age >60 new onset
Barrett esophagus: intestinal metaplasia → dysplasia → adenocarcinoma risk
Long-term PPI risks: C. diff, osteoporosis, hypomagnesemia, B12 deficiency, CKD (try to use lowest dose/shortest duration)
H. pylori: test and treat if found
Avoid: caffeine, alcohol, chocolate, fatty foods, mint, late meals
description: A chronic digestive condition where stomach acid frequently flows back into the esophagus, causing heartburn, regurgitation, and potential damage to the esophageal lining.

name: Peptic Ulcer Disease
signs:
Epigastric pain/burning
Gastric ulcer: pain worsened by eating
Duodenal ulcer: pain relieved by eating, worse 2-3h after meals
Nausea
Bloating
Melena or hematemesis (if bleeding)
Weight loss
Perforation: sudden severe pain, rigidity
treatment:
1. PPI therapy (first-line)
2. H. pylori eradication if positive
3. Stop NSAIDs/aspirin if possible
4. EGD for bleeding ulcer (epinephrine injection, clips, cautery)
5. Surgery for perforation, refractory bleeding, obstruction
6. Repeat EGD for gastric ulcers to confirm healing and rule out malignancy
meds:
Omeprazole 40mg daily x4-8 weeks
H. pylori triple therapy: PPI + clarithromycin 500mg BID + amoxicillin 1g BID x14 days
Bismuth quadruple: PPI + bismuth + metronidazole + tetracycline x14 days
Sucralfate 1g QID
Misoprostol 200mcg QID (NSAID ulcer prevention)
IV PPI 80mg bolus then 8mg/hr (bleeding ulcer)
ddx: GERD, Gastric cancer, Gastritis, Functional dyspepsia, Pancreatitis, Biliary colic, Zollinger-Ellison syndrome, Mesenteric ischemia
labs:
EGD with biopsies (gold standard)
H. pylori testing: stool antigen, urea breath test, or biopsy-based
CBC (anemia if chronic bleeding)
BMP
Fasting gastrin level if recurrent/refractory (Zollinger-Ellison)
CXR if perforation suspected (free air under diaphragm)
CT abdomen (perforation, complications)
notes:
H. pylori causes ~60% of gastric and ~90% of duodenal ulcers
NSAIDs: second most common cause
Confirm H. pylori eradication 4 weeks after treatment completion
Gastric ulcers: always biopsy (rule out malignancy)
Zollinger-Ellison: refractory/multiple ulcers, diarrhea, fasting gastrin >1000
Forrest classification for bleeding ulcers predicts rebleeding risk
description: Open sores that develop on the inner lining of the stomach or upper small intestine, most commonly caused by H. pylori infection or NSAID use.

name: Acute Cholecystitis
signs:
RUQ pain (persistent, >6 hours)
Murphy sign (inspiratory arrest with RUQ palpation)
Fever
Nausea/vomiting
RUQ tenderness and guarding
Referred pain to right shoulder (Kehr sign)
treatment:
1. NPO, IV fluids
2. IV antibiotics
3. Pain control (NSAIDs, opioids)
4. Cholecystectomy within 72h (early preferred)
5. Percutaneous cholecystostomy if not surgical candidate
6. ERCP if concurrent choledocholithiasis
meds:
Piperacillin/tazobactam 3.375g IV q6h
Ceftriaxone 2g IV + metronidazole 500mg IV q8h
Ketorolac 30mg IV q6h
Morphine 2-4mg IV q4h PRN
Ondansetron 4mg IV PRN
ddx: Biliary colic, Cholangitis, Peptic ulcer disease, Pancreatitis, Hepatitis, RLL pneumonia, Appendicitis, Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome
labs:
RUQ ultrasound (gallstones, wall thickening >4mm, pericholecystic fluid, sonographic Murphy)
CBC (leukocytosis)
LFTs, lipase
Bilirubin and ALP (elevated suggests CBD stone)
HIDA scan if US inconclusive (non-filling of gallbladder = positive)
CT abdomen if complications suspected
notes:
90% calculous (gallstones), 10% acalculous (ICU patients, HIV, DM)
Tokyo Guidelines severity: Grade I (mild), II (moderate - WBC >18K, mass, >72h), III (severe - organ dysfunction)
Complications: gangrenous cholecystitis, perforation, cholecystoenteric fistula, gallstone ileus
Early cholecystectomy (<72h) preferred over delayed
Acalculous cholecystitis: higher morbidity/mortality, often in critically ill patients
description: Acute inflammation of the gallbladder, usually caused by gallstones blocking the cystic duct, resulting in severe right upper abdominal pain and requiring surgical removal.

name: Diverticulitis
signs:
LLQ abdominal pain
Fever
Nausea/vomiting
Change in bowel habits (constipation or diarrhea)
Abdominal tenderness and guarding
Palpable mass (phlegmon or abscess)
Rectal bleeding (usually mild)
treatment:
1. Uncomplicated: outpatient antibiotics or observation
2. Complicated (abscess, perforation, fistula): hospitalization, IV antibiotics
3. CT-guided percutaneous drainage for abscess >3cm
4. Surgery for perforation with peritonitis, failure of medical management, fistula
5. Elective sigmoid colectomy after recovery from complicated episode
6. Colonoscopy 6-8 weeks after resolution
meds:
Ciprofloxacin 500mg BID + metronidazole 500mg TID x7-10 days (outpatient)
Amoxicillin/clavulanate 875/125mg BID (alternative)
Piperacillin/tazobactam 3.375g IV q6h (inpatient)
Acetaminophen 1g q6h (avoid NSAIDs)
Mesalamine (prevention - limited evidence)
High-fiber diet after resolution
ddx: Colon cancer, IBD, Appendicitis (right-sided), IBS, Ischemic colitis, Ovarian pathology, UTI, PID
labs:
CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast (pericolonic fat stranding, wall thickening, abscess, free air)
CBC (leukocytosis)
CRP elevated
Urinalysis (rule out UTI, detect colovesical fistula)
Blood cultures if septic
Colonoscopy after resolution (rule out malignancy - NOT during acute episode)
notes:
Most common: left-sided (sigmoid) in Western countries; right-sided more common in Asian populations
Hinchey classification: I-pericolic abscess, II-pelvic abscess, III-purulent peritonitis, IV-fecal peritonitis
Recent evidence: uncomplicated diverticulitis may not require antibiotics (DIABOLO trial)
Prevention: high-fiber diet, exercise, normal weight
Colonoscopy 6-8 weeks after to rule out underlying malignancy (found in 1-2%)
description: Inflammation or infection of small pouches (diverticula) that can form in the walls of the large intestine, most commonly causing left lower abdominal pain and fever.

name: Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis
signs:
Abdominal pain and tenderness
Fever
Altered mental status
Worsening ascites
Ileus
Hypotension
May be subtle or asymptomatic in cirrhotics
treatment:
1. Diagnostic paracentesis in ALL cirrhotics admitted with ascites
2. Empiric IV antibiotics immediately if PMN >250/mm3
3. IV albumin to prevent hepatorenal syndrome
4. Repeat paracentesis at 48h to confirm improvement
5. Long-term antibiotic prophylaxis after first episode
6. Liver transplant evaluation
meds:
Cefotaxime 2g IV q8h x5-7 days (first-line)
Ceftriaxone 2g IV daily (alternative)
Albumin 1.5g/kg day 1 + 1g/kg day 3
Norfloxacin 400mg daily (secondary prophylaxis)
TMP/SMX DS daily (prophylaxis alternative)
Ciprofloxacin 500mg daily (prophylaxis)
ddx: Secondary peritonitis (perforation), Peritoneal carcinomatosis, Tuberculous peritonitis, Pancreatitis, Hepatorenal syndrome
labs:
Paracentesis: cell count (PMN >250/mm3 = diagnostic), culture (blood culture bottles at bedside), protein, albumin, glucose, LDH
SAAG >1.1 (portal hypertension-related ascites)
Blood cultures
CBC, BMP, LFTs
Lactate
UA (rule out UTI as source)
notes:
Diagnostic threshold: ascitic fluid PMN >250/mm3 (start antibiotics before culture results)
Most common organisms: E. coli, Klebsiella, Streptococcus
Secondary peritonitis clues: polymicrobial, protein >1g/dL, glucose <50, LDH > serum
Albumin reduces hepatorenal syndrome from 30% to 10% (Sort trial)
Prophylaxis indications: prior SBP, ascitic protein <1.5 + renal dysfunction or liver failure, GI bleed
description: A bacterial infection of the ascitic fluid in the abdomen that occurs in patients with liver disease, often presenting subtly but requiring urgent antibiotic treatment.

name: Mesenteric Ischemia
signs:
Severe abdominal pain out of proportion to exam (classic)
Nausea/vomiting/diarrhea
Bloody stools (late sign)
Abdominal distension
Peritoneal signs (late - indicates necrosis)
History of AFib or vascular disease
Rapid clinical deterioration
treatment:
1. Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation
2. Broad-spectrum antibiotics
3. Emergent CTA of abdomen
4. Anticoagulation with heparin
5. Surgical embolectomy or bypass for arterial occlusion
6. Endovascular thrombectomy/thrombolysis
7. Exploratory laparotomy if peritonitis or bowel necrosis suspected
8. Second-look laparotomy at 24-48h
meds:
Heparin 80u/kg IV bolus then 18u/kg/hr
Piperacillin/tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h
Normal saline aggressive resuscitation
Papaverine 30-60mg/hr intra-arterial (vasospasm)
Avoid vasopressors if possible (worsen ischemia)
Morphine IV for pain control
ddx: Acute pancreatitis, Bowel obstruction, Perforated viscus, Aortic dissection, AAA rupture, Acute cholecystitis, Volvulus
labs:
CTA abdomen (filling defect in mesenteric vessels - sensitivity >95%)
Lactate elevated (late finding)
CBC (leukocytosis, hemoconcentration)
BMP (metabolic acidosis)
D-dimer elevated
Amylase/lipase
Abdominal XR (pneumatosis intestinalis, portal venous gas = late/ominous)
Conventional angiography (diagnostic and therapeutic)
notes:
Types: arterial embolism (50%, usually SMA from cardiac source), arterial thrombosis (25%), venous thrombosis (5-15%), non-occlusive (NOMI, 20%)
Pain out of proportion to exam is classic early finding
Lactate elevation is a late finding indicating necrosis (don't wait for it)
Mortality 50-80% - early diagnosis is critical
NOMI: ICU patients on vasopressors, treat with papaverine and optimize hemodynamics
Acute on chronic: collaterals from celiac/IMA may provide partial perfusion
description: A potentially fatal condition where blood flow to the intestines is suddenly reduced, causing severe abdominal pain and bowel damage that requires emergency treatment.

name: Acute Liver Failure
signs:
Jaundice
Encephalopathy (confusion, asterixis, coma)
Coagulopathy
RUQ tenderness
Hepatomegaly or shrinking liver
Ascites
Hypoglycemia
Cerebral edema
Multi-organ failure
treatment:
1. N-acetylcysteine (for acetaminophen AND non-acetaminophen ALF)
2. Emergent liver transplant evaluation
3. ICU care with ICP monitoring if grade 3-4 encephalopathy
4. Treat cerebral edema (mannitol, hypertonic saline)
5. Correct coagulopathy only if active bleeding or procedures
6. Continuous glucose monitoring
7. Avoid sedation if possible
meds:
N-acetylcysteine 150mg/kg IV over 1h, then 50mg/kg over 4h, then 100mg/kg over 16h
Mannitol 0.5-1g/kg IV (cerebral edema)
3% hypertonic saline (target Na 145-150 for elevated ICP)
Lactulose 30mL q2h (encephalopathy)
Vitamin K 10mg IV
Dextrose 10% infusion (hypoglycemia)
PPI for stress ulcer prophylaxis
ddx: Acute hepatitis (viral, alcoholic, autoimmune), Budd-Chiari syndrome, HELLP syndrome, Acute fatty liver of pregnancy, Wilson disease, Mushroom poisoning (Amanita)
labs:
LFTs (massive elevation: AST/ALT often >3000 in acetaminophen)
INR (key prognostic marker)
Ammonia level
Acetaminophen level
Viral hepatitis panel (A, B, C, E)
Autoimmune markers (ANA, ASMA, IgG)
Ceruloplasmin, copper (Wilson disease)
Pregnancy test
Lactate
Arterial ammonia
King's College criteria for transplant listing
notes:
Definition: INR >1.5 + encephalopathy + no pre-existing liver disease + illness <26 weeks
Most common cause in US/UK: acetaminophen toxicity
King's College criteria (acetaminophen): pH <7.3 or INR >6.5 + creatinine >3.4 + grade 3-4 HE
Grade of encephalopathy: 1-altered mood, 2-drowsy/asterixis, 3-stupor, 4-coma
NAC benefits non-acetaminophen ALF as well (improves transplant-free survival)
Hypercoagulable despite elevated INR (balanced hemostatic defect)
description: Rapid deterioration of liver function in a person without pre-existing liver disease, causing coagulopathy, jaundice, and brain swelling, often requiring emergency liver transplantation.

name: Celiac Disease
signs:
Chronic diarrhea (steatorrhea)
Abdominal bloating and pain
Weight loss
Iron deficiency anemia
Fatigue
Dermatitis herpetiformis
Osteoporosis
Failure to thrive (children)
Peripheral neuropathy
Dental enamel defects
treatment:
1. Strict lifelong gluten-free diet (wheat, barley, rye)
2. Dietitian consultation
3. Correct nutritional deficiencies (iron, B12, folate, calcium, vitamin D)
4. DEXA scan for osteoporosis
5. Monitor adherence with serologies
6. Repeat biopsy if symptoms persist despite diet
7. Screen first-degree relatives
meds:
Iron supplementation (ferrous sulfate 325mg daily)
Calcium 1200mg + Vitamin D 1000IU daily
Folate 1mg daily
Multivitamin
Dapsone 25-50mg daily (dermatitis herpetiformis)
No pharmacologic treatment for celiac itself - diet only
Budesonide (refractory celiac, short-term)
ddx: IBS, IBD, Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth (SIBO), Tropical sprue, Lactose intolerance, Whipple disease, Pancreatic insufficiency, Giardiasis
labs:
tTG-IgA (tissue transglutaminase - best screening test)
Total IgA (IgA deficiency causes false-negative tTG)
Endomysial antibody (EMA) - confirmatory
Small bowel biopsy (villous atrophy, crypt hyperplasia, intraepithelial lymphocytes) - gold standard
CBC (iron deficiency anemia)
Iron studies, B12, folate, vitamin D, calcium
DEXA scan
HLA-DQ2/DQ8 (99% have one - good negative predictive value)
notes:
Must be on gluten-containing diet during testing (false negatives if already GF)
HLA-DQ2 (95%) or DQ8 (5%) - absence essentially rules out celiac
Associated: Type 1 DM, thyroid disease, Down syndrome, Turner syndrome, IgA deficiency
Refractory celiac: persistent symptoms/villous atrophy despite strict GF diet >12 months
Dermatitis herpetiformis: pathognomonic skin manifestation, granular IgA at dermal papillae
Small increased risk of intestinal T-cell lymphoma and small bowel adenocarcinoma
description: An autoimmune disorder where eating gluten triggers an immune response that damages the small intestine's lining, impairing nutrient absorption and causing digestive symptoms.

name: Irritable Bowel Syndrome
signs:
Recurrent abdominal pain related to defecation
Altered bowel habits (diarrhea, constipation, or mixed)
Bloating and distension
Mucus in stool
Symptoms worse with stress
No nocturnal symptoms
No weight loss or rectal bleeding (alarm features absent)
treatment:
1. Reassurance and patient education
2. Dietary modifications (low FODMAP diet)
3. Fiber supplementation for IBS-C
4. Antispasmodics for pain
5. Loperamide for IBS-D
6. Psychological therapy (CBT, gut-directed hypnotherapy)
7. Treat associated anxiety/depression
meds:
Psyllium fiber 5-10g daily (IBS-C)
Dicyclomine 10-20mg QID PRN (antispasmodic)
Loperamide 2-4mg PRN (IBS-D)
Linaclotide 290mcg daily (IBS-C)
Rifaximin 550mg TID x14 days (IBS-D)
Alosetron 0.5-1mg BID (severe IBS-D, women only)
Amitriptyline 10-25mg at bedtime (pain)
SSRI (comorbid anxiety/depression)
ddx: IBD, Celiac disease, Colorectal cancer, Microscopic colitis, SIBO, Lactose intolerance, Endometriosis, Hyperthyroidism
labs:
Diagnosis of exclusion using Rome IV criteria
CBC, CRP/ESR (normal in IBS)
Celiac serologies (tTG-IgA)
Fecal calprotectin (normal in IBS, elevated in IBD)
TSH
Stool studies if diarrhea-predominant
Colonoscopy if alarm features or age >45
Hydrogen breath test (lactose intolerance, SIBO)
notes:
Rome IV criteria: recurrent abdominal pain >=1 day/week x3 months, associated with >=2 of: related to defecation, change in frequency, change in form
Subtypes: IBS-D (diarrhea), IBS-C (constipation), IBS-M (mixed), IBS-U (unsubtyped)
Alarm features requiring investigation: >50yo onset, rectal bleeding, weight loss, nocturnal symptoms, family hx CRC/IBD, anemia
Low FODMAP diet effective in 50-70% but restrictive
Gut-brain axis dysfunction is central to pathophysiology
description: A common functional gastrointestinal disorder causing recurrent abdominal pain and altered bowel habits without any identifiable structural or biochemical abnormality.

name: Gastroparesis
signs:
Nausea and vomiting (often of undigested food)
Early satiety
Postprandial fullness
Bloating
Abdominal pain
Weight loss
Erratic blood glucose (diabetics)
Succussion splash
treatment:
1. Dietary modifications (small, frequent, low-fat, low-fiber meals)
2. Prokinetic agents
3. Antiemetics for symptom relief
4. Optimize glycemic control (diabetic gastroparesis)
5. Gastric electrical stimulation (Enterra) for refractory cases
6. Pyloric interventions (G-POEM, pyloroplasty)
7. Jejunostomy tube if unable to maintain nutrition
meds:
Metoclopramide 5-10mg PO/IV TID (max 12 weeks, risk of tardive dyskinesia)
Domperidone 10mg TID (not FDA-approved, available via expanded access)
Erythromycin 250mg IV/PO TID (short-term, tachyphylaxis)
Ondansetron 4-8mg TID PRN
Promethazine 12.5-25mg PO/PR q6h PRN
Nortriptyline 10-25mg at bedtime (pain/nausea)
ddx: Gastric outlet obstruction (mechanical), Cyclic vomiting syndrome, Functional dyspepsia, Eating disorders, SMA syndrome, Rumination syndrome, Cannabis hyperemesis syndrome
labs:
Gastric emptying scintigraphy (>10% retained at 4h = abnormal) - gold standard
EGD (rule out mechanical obstruction)
Small bowel follow-through
HbA1c (diabetic control)
TSH
BMP
CBC
Wireless motility capsule (SmartPill)
Antroduodenal manometry (specialized centers)
notes:
Most common causes: diabetic (30%), post-surgical (13%), idiopathic (35%)
Stop medications that delay gastric emptying before testing: opioids, GLP-1 agonists, anticholinergics
4h gastric emptying study: gold standard (standardized low-fat egg white meal)
Metoclopramide: FDA black box warning for tardive dyskinesia - limit to 12 weeks
GLP-1 receptor agonists (semaglutide) can cause/worsen gastroparesis
G-POEM: endoscopic pyloromyotomy, emerging treatment option
description: A condition where the stomach takes too long to empty its contents due to impaired muscle contractions, causing nausea, vomiting, and bloating, most often associated with diabetes.
